title: Geometric aspects of spin transport in magnetic multilayers
abstract: We discuss spin-dependent transfer-matrix formalism applied to magnetic multilayers in geometric terms. Starting from the stationary Schrödinger equation rewritten as a first-order spatial evolution problem, we interpret the transfer matrix as a path-ordered exponential and relate its matching-matrix construction to a noncompact group constraint. We thenconnect the induced Möbius action on reflection matrices to an Iwasawa decomposition, identify Weyl-chamber variables as the minimal noncompact transport invariants, and show how torque-related spin structures arise from compact-sector and commutator contributions. A sequence of multilayer examples illustrates the transition from pure spin filtering to controlled spin-orbit mixing and the resulting deformation of Weyl-chamber trajectories. We finally comment on the extension to higher-dimensional internal spaces relevant to orbital transport and realistic calculations.
summary: This work develops a geometric theory for spin transport in magnetic multilayers by mapping the transfer matrix formalism onto the structure of noncompact Lie groups. It uses Iwasawa decomposition and Weyl-chamber variables to characterize spin filtering, while commutator analysis rigorously links the non-commutativity of spin filters to the generation of spin-transfer torque components.
main_problem: To establish a rigorous geometric framework for describing spin-dependent electron transport through magnetic multilayers.
main_result: The transport dynamics are characterized by a path-ordered exponential (transfer matrix) whose action can be decomposed using Iwasawa theory, revealing minimal transport invariants (Weyl-chamber variables) and linking non-commutativity to spin-torque generation.
method: The authors employ advanced group theory, specifically interpreting the transfer matrix as an element of a noncompact Lie group, utilizing Iwasawa decomposition, and analyzing commutator structures via the Baker-Campbell-Hausdorff expansion.
model_or_system: The system is magnetic multilayers (e.g., FM/NM/FM stacks) where spin-resolved electron transport is governed by the stationary Schrödinger equation. The model incorporates spin-orbit coupling (SOC) and is analyzed using transfer matrices derived from first-order spatial evolution equations.
key_observables: Weyl-chamber variables ($\lambda_i$), Spin–Mueller–Jones tensor components, and the commutator norm $\|[r'_{13}, r_{35}]\|$ which quantifies out-of-plane spin current.
important_parameters: SOC strength ($\alpha_{so}$), spacer thickness, and the relative magnetization angles ($	heta$) between adjacent ferromagnetic layers.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the transfer matrix formalism being equivalent to a path-ordered exponential, and the use of Iwasawa decomposition for the underlying Lie group structure.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the evolution of Weyl coordinates ($\lambda_1, \lambda_2$) as a function of analyzer angle or spacer thickness, demonstrating the separation of compact (spin rotation) and noncompact (filtering) information, and showing the deformation of the Weyl trajectory due to SOC.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing the transfer matrix formalism, then applying group theory (Möbius/Iwasawa) to decompose the action. It subsequently analyzes the role of SOC and non-commuting reflections, culminating in the structural derivation of spin-torque components via commutator analysis.
why_interesting: The deep integration of geometric group theory (Iwasawa, Weyl chambers) into condensed matter transport physics provides a powerful, unifying mathematical language for understanding complex spin-filtering phenomena, analogous to how geometric methods are used in quantum optics or quantum field theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 3. Weyl-chamber trajectory for the single-FM thickness sweep. The diagonal λ1 = λ2 corresponds to spin-independent radial filtering.
FIG. 1. Model geometry for the single-FM example. Identical nonmagnetic leads are attached to a single ferromagnetic layer whose thickness is swept.

FIG. 5. Spin-current components in the spacer of the FM/NM/FM stack as the analyser angle is swept. The lower panel compares the out-of-plane spin current component with the noncommutativity diagnostic ∥[r′ 13, r35]∥.
FIG. 6. Weyl coordinates for the FM/NM/FM stack as a function of analyser angle. The splitting λ1 −λ2 tracks the anisotropic part of the radial filtering response.

Spintronics.note: The new paper directly models spin-dependent electron transport in magnetic multilayers using transfer matrices and SOC, which falls squarely within the domain of spintronics, particularly concerning spin-torque generation in magnetic heterostructures.
